meta.title: Daily Reflection Tree
meta.description: A deterministic end-of-day reflection tool guiding employees through Locus of Control, Contribution Orientation, and Radius of Concern.

nodes.text: Good evening. Before you close the day, let's take five minutes to look at it honestly. No grades. No judgements. Just a quiet look at how you showed up.
If today were a headline, what would it say about you?
options: I pushed through and made progress
Things kept getting in my way
I held steady despite the chaos
Today happened to me more than I happened to it

match: I pushed through and made progress
I held steady despite the chaos
Things kept getting in my way
Today happened to me more than I happened to it

nodes.text: You said "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When something went sideways today, what was your instinct?
options: I looked for what I could still control
I adapted and found another route
I told myself it would pass
I pushed harder on the same thing even if it wasn't working

nodes.text: You said "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When things got difficult today, where did your attention go first?
options: To what other people should have done differently
To bad timing or bad luck
To wishing circumstances were different
To what small thing I could still change

match: I looked for what I could still control
I adapted and found another route
I told myself it would pass
I pushed harder on the same thing even if it wasn't working
To what other people should have done differently
To bad timing or bad luck
To wishing circumstances were different
To what small thing I could still change

nodes.text: Think of the hardest moment of your day. Was there a choice you made — even a small one — that shaped how it went?
options: Yes — I can name it clearly
Maybe — I hadn't really thought about it
I'm not sure I had any real choice
The situation didn't leave me much room

match: Yes — I can name it clearly
Maybe — I hadn't really thought about it
I'm not sure I had any real choice
The situation didn't leave me much room

nodes.text: Looking back now with a cooler head — was there a moment today where you had more influence than you realized at the time?
options: Yes, probably — I see it now
Possibly, but I'm still not sure
Not really — the situation was beyond me
I'll think about that later

match: Yes, probably — I see it now
Possibly, but I'm still not sure
Not really — the situation was beyond me
I'll think about that later

nodes.text: You stayed in the driver's seat today. Not because everything went your way — but because you looked for what you could still shape. That's not optimism. That's a skill. It compounds.
Seeing your own agency after the fact is actually the first step to seeing it in the moment. That gap — between event and reflection — is where growth lives. You're in it right now.

Hard days pull the camera outward — to what others did, what circumstances allowed. That's human. But somewhere in there, you made at least one call. You may not have liked your options. You still chose. That matters.
Now let's shift from how you handled things — to what you gave today.
Think about your energy today — where was most of it directed?
options: Getting my own work done well
Helping others get their work done
Making sure I got credit for what I did
Making things easier for my team

match: Helping others get their work done
Making things easier for my team
Getting my own work done well
Making sure I got credit for what I did

nodes.text: You gave energy outward today. Was there a moment you did something useful that nobody will probably notice?
options: Yes — I can think of one right now
Maybe — I did things that might go unnoticed
Probably not — I mostly did what was expected
I'm not sure I did anything extra

nodes.text: Did you feel at any point today that you were working harder than others or deserved more recognition than you got?
options: Honestly, yes — it felt unfair
A little, but I let it go
Not really — I focused on the work itself
I try not to compare myself to others

match: Yes — I can think of one right now
Maybe — I did things that might go unnoticed
Probably not — I mostly did what was expected
I'm not sure I did anything extra
Honestly, yes — it felt unfair
A little, but I let it go
Not really — I focused on the work itself
I try not to compare myself to others

nodes.text: Is there something you could have offered today — a hand, a word, a bit of knowledge — that you held back or didn't think to give?
options: Yes, I can think of something
Maybe — there was an opportunity I passed
Not really — I was stretched too thin
I genuinely gave what I had

match: Yes, I can think of something
Maybe — there was an opportunity I passed
Not really — I was stretched too thin
I genuinely gave what I had

nodes.text: You spent some of today's energy on others. That's not nothing — it's actually rare. Most people run on empty and still find ways to give. You were one of them today.
Noticing the moment you could have given — and didn't — is the first step to giving next time. That's not a failure. That's the gap that improvement lives in. Most people never even see it.
One more lens. Let's zoom out — from what you gave, to who you were thinking about.
When you replay today in your mind right now, who shows up in the story?
options: Mostly me — my work, my stress, my goals
Me and a few specific people I interacted with
My team broadly — we were all in it together
People beyond my team — customers, the company, someone affected by our work

match: Mostly me — my work, my stress, my goals
Me and a few specific people I interacted with
My team broadly — we were all in it together
People beyond my team — customers, the company, someone affected by our work

nodes.text: That's honest. When you were deep in your own work today, was there a moment you noticed how it connected to something or someone beyond you?
options: Briefly — I thought about who would use what I was building
Not really — I was too focused to look up
I'm not sure my work connects to much beyond my tasks
I knew it mattered but I didn't think about who specifically

nodes.text: You thought about a few people today. Was there someone outside your immediate circle — a customer, another team, someone downstream — who crossed your mind?
options: Yes — I thought about how my work affected them
Briefly, but not deeply
Not really — it was all pretty local
I didn't have bandwidth to think that far

nodes.text: You were thinking beyond yourself today. Was there a moment you made a decision with someone else's reality in mind — not just your own?
options: Yes — I can think of a specific moment
I think so, though it was implicit
It was more of a general awareness than a specific decision
I'm not sure it influenced my actual decisions

match: Briefly — I thought about who would use what I was building
I knew it mattered but I didn't think about who specifically
Not really — I was too focused to look up
I'm not sure my work connects to much beyond my tasks
Yes — I thought about how my work affected them
Briefly, but not deeply
Not really — it was all pretty local
I didn't have bandwidth to think that far
Yes — I can think of a specific moment
I think so, though it was implicit
It was more of a general awareness than a specific decision
I'm not sure it influenced my actual decisions

nodes.text: Deep focus is a real skill. The world also gets richer when you occasionally look up. Tomorrow, try to name one person — outside your immediate tasks — who your work touches. Just naming them changes how you work.

You're starting to feel the edges of your impact — where your work ends and others begin. That awareness is the beginning of working with a wider purpose. It doesn't require grand gestures. It just requires knowing who you're working for.

You thought beyond yourself today in a real way. That's Maslow's peak — not because it's idealistic, but because it's the most durable source of motivation there is. People who work for others don't burn out the way people who work only for themselves do.

axis1.internal: You saw your hand in today's events, even when things were hard.
axis1.external: Today's story felt written by others. There's always a sentence you authored — it's worth finding.

axis2.contribution: You gave energy outward. That's not a small thing.
axis2.entitlement: Your focus was on what today owed you. Worth asking what you offered in return.

axis3.self: Your frame was close and personal today.
axis3.mid: You were beginning to see beyond yourself.
axis3.wide: You held a wide view — your work in context of something larger.

closing.internal_contribution_wide: A strong day. You saw your agency, gave without needing to be asked, and kept others in frame. That's the whole thing.
closing.internal_contribution_mid: Solid footing today. Your agency and generosity are real. The next frontier is widening who you hold in mind.
closing.internal_entitlement_wide: Interesting contrast today — you owned your actions but were tracking what you deserved. Both things can be true. The question is which one drives you.
closing.external_contribution_wide: You gave genuinely and thought broadly, even on a day where you felt pushed around. That's resilience wearing a quiet disguise.
closing.default: Every day has a lesson. The fact that you're here, looking at it honestly, is the beginning of tomorrow being different.

nodes.text: Rest well. Tomorrow is a fresh start.
